questions.question: The `chmod 777 file` command gives read, write, and execute permissions to everyone.
The `grep` command can only search one file at a time.
questions.explanation: `grep` accepts multiple file arguments or wildcards.

questions.question: A Bash function can return a string value using the `return` keyword.
questions.explanation: `return` returns an integer exit code. To output a string, use `echo`.

questions.question: The command `ps aux` shows only processes owned by the current user.
questions.explanation: `ps aux` shows all processes on the system (a = all users, x = processes without terminal).

questions.question: The `sudo apt update` command upgrades installed packages to their latest versions.
questions.explanation: `update` refreshes the package index; `upgrade` actually installs updates.

questions.question: Redirecting a command's output with `>` will append to the file if it already exists.
The `kill -9` signal can be caught or ignored by a process.
questions.explanation: SIGKILL (9) cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored.

questions.question: The `cron` daemon checks for jobs to run every second.
questions.explanation: Cron has a granularity of one minute.
